perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall pay Club the Annual Fee for each Contract Year of this Agreement in six (6) equal installments, each due on or prior to the 1st of each month between June and November of the applicable Contract Year.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall pay Club the Annual Fee for each Contract Year of this Agreement in six (6) equal installments, with the full Annual Fee due on December 31st of the applicable Contract Year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment schedule to a single payment due at the end of the year contradicts Florida Statute 215.422 which requires invoices to be paid promptly.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 215.422 mandates that state agencies (and entities contracting with them) adhere to prompt payment standards, generally requiring payment within a specified timeframe (often around 30 days) of receiving a correct invoice. Changing the payment terms to a single, end-of-year payment conflicts with the underlying principle of prompt payment, potentially delaying revenue recognition and creating cash flow issues. It can be argued that a sports team is a government entity, subject to state prompt payment laws.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XIV > Chapter 215 > Section 422 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XIV TAXATION AND FINANCE Chapter 215 FINANCIAL MATTERS: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 422 Payments, warrants, and invoices; processing time limits; dispute resolution; agency or judicial branch compliance. F.S. 215.422 215.422 Payments, warrants, and invoices; processing time limits; dispute resolution; agency or judicial branch compliance. — (1) An invoice submitted to an agency of the state or the judicial branch, required by law to be filed with the Chief Financial Officer, shall be recorded in the financial systems of the state, approved for payment by the agency or the judicial branch, and filed with the Chief Financial Officer not later than 20 days after receipt of the invoice and receipt, inspection, and approval of the goods or services, except that in the case of a bona fide dispute the invoice recorded in the financial systems of the state shall contain a statement of the dispute and authorize payment only in the amount not disputed. The Chief Financial Officer may establish dollar thresholds and other criteria for all invoices and may delegate to a state agency or the judicial branch responsibility for maintaining the official invoices and documents for invoices which do not exceed the thresholds or which meet the established criteria. Such records shall be maintained in accordance with the requirements established by the Secretary of State. The transmission of an approved invoice recorded in the financial systems of the state to the Chief Financial Officer shall constitute filing of a request for payment of invoices for which the Chief Financial Officer has delegated to an agency custody of official records. Approval and inspection of goods or services shall take no longer than 5 working days unless the bid specifications, purchase order, or contract specifies otherwise. If an invoice filed within the 20-day period is returned by the Department of Financial Services because of an error, it shall nevertheless be deemed timely filed. The 20-day filing requirement may be waived in whole or in part by the Department of Financial Services on a showing of exceptional circumstances in accordance with rules and regulations of the department. For the purposes of determining t
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the payment schedule from six installments to a single payment at the end of the year. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute 215.422 mandates prompt payment standards. The Perturbation Explanation states the change to a single payment contradicts Florida Statute 215.422. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines that agencies should process and file invoices within 20 days of receipt and approval of goods or services. By changing the payment terms to a single end-of-year payment, the contract contradicts the prompt payment requirements of Florida Statute 215.422.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be considered given when delivered personally or by electronic mail (confirmed by one of the other permissible methods of giving notice hereunder), one business day after being sent by a nationally recognized overnight courier, or three business days after being mailed by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid and return receipt requested, to the parties at the following addresses (or at such other address as a party may specify by notice to the other):
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement shall be considered given only when sent via carrier pigeon, and only if the pigeon successfully arrives at the recipient's location.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to carrier pigeon is not recognized by law and would be unenforceable, thus contradicting modern legal standards for notification.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UETA and similar state laws validate electronic records and signatures, treating them as equivalent to paper documents. By specifying only carrier pigeon, the changed text contradicts the acceptance of electronic or other commercially reasonable means of communication recognized by UETA and modern contract law. Section 15 specifically discusses admissibility in evidence of electronic records and signatures.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that notice can *only* be given via carrier pigeon. This directly contradicts the original text which allows for multiple methods of communication, including electronic mail and overnight courier. The Law Explanation states that the UETA validates electronic records and signatures. While the provided scraped snippets do not explicitly mention the UETA, the Law Explanation's reference to it suggests that the exclusive use of carrier pigeons contradicts modern legal standards that accept electronic communication. The perturbation explanation confirms that restricting notice to carrier pigeon is not recognized by law, and therefore unenforceable. Thus, the changed text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement (the "Term") shall commence as of April 1, 2018 (the "Effective Date") and shall expire upon the later of: (a) the conclusion of the 2022/23 NFL season and (b) the last day in February, 2023 (such expiration date, the "Scheduled Expiration Date"), unless sooner terminated pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement (the "Term") shall commence as of April 1, 2018 (the "Effective Date") and shall expire upon the death of the Jacksonville Jaguars team owner, regardless of any other provision within this agreement, unless sooner terminated pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Making the contract term contingent on the death of a person creates an indefinite and potentially perpetual contract, which could violate rules against restraints on alienation or create uncertainty regarding contract enforcement. It also might violate general principles of contract certainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 77 addresses illusory promises and requires consideration to support a contract. While not directly about death contingencies, making the contract's end date dependent on an unpredictable event like death introduces uncertainty that undermines the definiteness required for valid consideration and contract enforceability. This also contradicts the fixed termination date. An NFL team cannot be owned by an estate so this may run counter to NFL rules as well.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a definite end date tied to the NFL season and a specific date in February 2023. The changed text replaces this with an indefinite end date tied to the death of the Jacksonville Jaguars team owner. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this creates an indefinite and potentially perpetual contract, creating uncertainty regarding contract enforcement. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the definiteness required for valid consideration and contract enforceability, contradicting the fixed termination date. Although there are no law snippets provided, the LLM explanation combined with the Perturbation Explanation and the text change clearly indicates a move away from a fixed, enforceable term, creating a significant contradiction.
